board.title: Programming Languages
board.author: Julius Boateng
board.description: Programming languages aren't reducible to syntax. They reflect different paradigms, ecosystems, and design philosophies. "A language that doesn't affect the way you think about programming is not worth knowing." — Alan Perlis

clues.question: Language prototyped in 10 days by Brendan Eich at Netscape; modern browsers have dedicated engines to execute it
Developed in 1958 for symbolic AI research; functional language known for recursion and symbolic expressions; short for List Processing
Early language backed by the U.S. Department of Defense; still used for banking and finance; short for Common Business-Oriented Language
Systems language known for high performance and memory safety without garbage collection; later sponsored by Mozilla
Early procedural language created by Niklaus Wirth at ETH Zürich; influential in structured programming and university-level computer science courses
Modern JVM language interoperable with Java; adds null safety, coroutines, and concise syntax; preferred for Android development
Created by Bjarne Stroustrup as "C with Classes"; an object-oriented extension of C used for high-performance applications (letters only)
Object-oriented language designed to be architecture-neutral and garbage-collected; known for "write once, run anywhere"
Dynamically typed language emphasizing readability and simplicity; widely used for machine learning; Zen of ______
Strongly typed language commissioned by the U.S. Department of Defense; based on Steelman requirements; named after Lovelace
Created by Apple to replace Objective-C; introduced cleaner syntax, type inference, and null safety for iOS development
Developed by IBM in 1957; one of the earliest major high-level languages; short for Formula Translating System
General-purpose scripting language widely used for early web development; notable for text processing, regular expressions, and automation
Programming language created in Japan with worldwide adoption; treats nearly all values as objects; designed for readability and developer ergonomics
Created at Dartmouth College to make computing accessible to students outside science; short for Beginner's All-purpose Symbolic Instruction Code
Created by Dennis Ritchie at Bell Labs in 1972; low-level systems language with direct memory access
Statically typed language created at Google known for goroutines and channels for lightweight concurrency
